coredata.dc:title: Reconfiguring solvation network and interfacial engineering of Zn metal anode with biomass carbon quantum dot
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Inc. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Yu, Yang
Lin, Li
Hu, Zhen-Yu
Liu, Yu-Hang
Sun, Lian-Shan
Liu, Wan-Qiang
Tian, Song-Lin
Liu, Wei
Wang, Dong
coredata.dc:description: The implementation of aqueous zinc-ion batteries (AZIBs) in energy storage systems has uneven Zn dendrites and side reactions seriously at the zinc anode and electrolyte interface. A novel green biomass carbon quantum dot (BCDs) additive influenced the hydrogen bonding network of the electrolyte, reduced water activity, and suppressed hydrogen evolution corrosion of the zinc anode. At the same time, we proposed a buffer redistribution effect that BCDs can act as the support of SO4 2−, and the linkage mechanism can further prolong the residence time of Zn2+ on SO4 2−-BCDs. This effectively allows more time for the adsorbed Zn2+ to migrate to the most stable position on Zn (0 0 2) and form a perfect pattern, it regulated the Zn2+ solvation network and interfacial chemistry of zinc anodes. In addition, the Zn||Zn-symmetric battery with a current density of 1 mA cm−2/1 mAh cm−2 can cycling 3450 h. And the constructed full Zn||PANI full battery can be stably cycled 5000 cycles at a current density of 0.5 and 5 A g−1. This study reveals the principle of maximizing zinc utilization solvate structural interface design, and highlights the commercial potential of BCDs additives in the design for efficient and durable AZIBs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Aqueous zinc-ion battery
Dendrite growth
Electrolyte
Zinc anode
BCDs additive

The authors extend their gratitude to Shiyanjia Lab form providing invaluable assistance with the TOF-SIMS analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of BCDs role in impacting the interface dynamics and the zinc deposition behaviors.

(a) XRD patterns of prepared BCDs. (b) PL emission spectra of BCDs/ZnSO4 electrolyte and 0.5 mg mL−1 BCDs solution. (c) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of 0.5 mg mL−1 BCDs (Illustration shows a photo of BCDs solution under fluorescent light). (d) FITR spectra of pure ZnSO4 electrolyte and ZnSO4 with different proportions electrolytes of BCDs. (e) Raman spectra of pure ZnSO4 electrolyte and ZnSO4 with different proportions electrolytes of BCDs. (f) 1H NMR spectra of pure ZnSO4 electrolyte and ZnSO4 with different proportions electrolytes of BCDs. (g) The theoretical the reaction at Zn (002) surface.

(a) Cyclic voltammetry curves of Zn||Cu battery with a scan rate of 1 mV s−1. (b) Coulombic efficiency tests of Zn||Cu cells and corresponding voltage profiles at various cycles after 50 cycles at 1 mA cm−2 with a capacity of 1 mAh·cm−2 in BCDs/ZnSO4 electrolyte. (c) Coulombic efficiency tests of Zn||Cu cells. (d) Tafel plots of Zn||Zn symmetric cells showing the corrosion on bare Zn with ZnSO4 and BCDs/ZnSO4 electrolytes. (e) EIS spectra of the Zn symmetric cells before/after 10 cycles with/without the BCDs additive. (f) Linear sweep voltammetry curves showing the on bare Zn with ZnSO4 and BCDs/ZnSO4 electrolytes. (g) Galvanostatic cycling of symmetrical cells at 1 mA cm−2 and 1 mAh cm−2 with ZnSO4 and BCDs/ZnSO4 electrolytes. (h) State-of-the-art performance in terms of current density and lifetime compared to carbon-based additives. The data obtained in ZnSO4 are provided for comparison.

(a) BCDs/ZnSO4 electrolyte and (b) ZnSO4 electrolyte In-situ optical microscope observation of the Zn deposition process (5, 10, 30, 60 min) in different electrolytes at 2 mA cm−2. (c) ZnSO4 electrolyte (d) BCDs/ZnSO4 electrolyte COMSOL simulation model of the current density electric field and Zinc ion concentration distributions. (e) The adsorption structure and electron locational function for BCDs, SO4 2− and Zn2+ on Zn (002) crystal plane. (f) The adsorption structure and electron locational function for BCDs-SO4 2−, SO4 2−-Zn2+, Zn2+-H2O.

(a) Comparison of XRD patterns (b) Raman spectrum (c) S 2p XPS spectra of Zn foils after 50 cycles at 1 mA cm−2 and 1 mAh cm−2 using ZnSO4 and BCDs/ZnSO4 electrolytes. Corresponding height distribution of CLSM images with (d) BCDs additive and (e) without BCDs additive (f) Confocal luminescence image of a Zn plate in BCDs/ZnSO4 electrolytes. (g) Three-dimensional view of distributions of Zn sheet after cycled in BCDs/ZnSO4 electrolytes in the TOF-SIMS sputtered volumes.

Full battery performance testing of Zn||PANI cells in BCDs/ZnSO4 and ZnSO4 electrolytes, respectively. (a) CV curves at a scan rate of 1 mV s−1. (b) Charge-discharge curves. (c) Capacities variation under different current densities, respectively. (d) The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy. (e) Cyclic stability and Coulombic efficiency comparison at 0.5 A g−1. (f) Cyclic stability and Coulombic efficiency comparison at 2 A g−1.

head.ce:title: Reconfiguring solvation network and interfacial engineering of Zn metal anode with biomass carbon quantum dot

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Changchun University of Science and Technology, Changchun, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering
Changchun University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry, Tonghua Normal University Tonghua, Jilin, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry, Tonghua Normal University Tonghua, Jilin, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Preparation and Applications of Environmental Friendly Materials of the Ministry of Education & College of Chemistry, Jilin Normal University, Changchun 130103, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Preparation and Applications of Environmental Friendly Materials of the Ministry of Education & College of Chemistry, Jilin Normal University, Changchun 130103, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Rare Earth Resource Utilization, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Changchun 130022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Rare Earth Resource Utilization

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Rare Earth Resource Utilization, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Changchun 130022, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Jilin University, Changchun 130013, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Jilin University, Changchun 130013, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We have innovatively designed an environmentally friendly natural biomass carbon quantum dots (BCDs) as an electrolyte additive. BCDs possesses abundant zincophilic groups and exhibits bright fluorescence, thereby achieving dual functionalities of zinc anode protection and fluorescence monitoring. For the first time, we introduced BCDs into the ZnSO4 electrolyte, where the activity of water molecules is suppressed due to hydrogen bonding interactions between BCD and H2O. This study elucidates the principle of maximizing zinc utilization through solvate structural interface design and underscores the commercial potential of BCDs additives in advancing aqueous zinc-ion batteries.

The implementation of aqueous zinc-ion batteries (AZIBs) in energy storage systems has uneven Zn dendrites and side reactions seriously at the zinc anode and electrolyte interface. A novel green biomass carbon quantum dot (BCDs) additive influenced the hydrogen bonding network of the electrolyte, reduced water activity, and suppressed hydrogen evolution corrosion of the zinc anode. At the same time, we proposed a buffer redistribution effect that BCDs can act as the support of SO4 2−, and the linkage mechanism can further prolong the residence time of Zn2+ on SO4 2−-BCDs. This effectively allows more time for the adsorbed Zn2+ to migrate to the most stable position on Zn (0 0 2) and form a perfect pattern, it regulated the Zn2+ solvation network and interfacial chemistry of zinc anodes. In addition, the Zn||Zn-symmetric battery with a current density of 1 mA cm−2/1 mAh cm−2 can cycling 3450 h. And the constructed full Zn||PANI full battery can be stably cycled 5000 cycles at a current density of 0.5 and 5 A g−1. This study reveals the principle of maximizing zinc utilization solvate structural interface design, and highlights the commercial potential of BCDs additives in the design for efficient and durable AZIBs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: The rapid ascent of emerging economies and evolving lifestyles has led to a significant surge in energy demand [1,2]. However, the exploitation and utilization of traditional energy sources have also resulted in severe environmental pollution. Accelerating the development and implementation of clean energy and renewable technologies to ensure sustainable energy supply and environmental protection [3]. Aqueous battery technology, characterized by its cost-effectiveness, inherent safety and environmental friendliness, has garnered extensive attention and in-depth research [4,5].

As a promising aqueous battery AZIBs have garnered significant attention, owing to their unique benefits, which include affordability and environmental compatibility. It makes that AZIBs become a rapidly growing electrochemical energy storage system [6–9]. Zn anode stands out as the most promising anode material for large-scale energy storage applications due to high theoretical specific capacity (820 mAh g−1) and suitable redox potential (−0.76 V vs SHE) [10–12]. However, the poor reversibility of the Zn anode and the low Coulombic efficiency (CE) led to water-induced side reactions and dendrite growth [13,14]. Specifically, uncontrolled Zn dendrite growth can exacerbate the “tip effect,” potentially penetrating the separator and causing short circuits during charge/discharge cycles. Additionally, the hydrogen evolution reaction (HER) in aqueous environments can lead to cell expansion and even explosion [15–17].

Consequently, there is imperative to develop effective strategies to mitigate these challenges, focusing on artificial interface layer construction, anode structure design, electrolyte regulation, and other approaches [18,19]. Among these, introducing electrolyte additives to regulate Zn anode interface reactions appears to be the most practical and effective approach [20,21]. The functional design of electrolyte additives holds significant potential for enhancing the stability of the Zn anode electrode interface [22–24]. Fundamentally, the interfacial stability of the Zn anode is influenced by the solvation structure of Zn2+ in the electrolyte and the behavior of Zn2+ diffusion-deposition on the Zn anode surface [25]. Therefore, reconfiguring the solvation structure of Zn2+ to minimize side reactions while promoting uniform deposition may be crucial for achieving stable Zn anode interfaces under ultra-long cycle life conditions in full batteries.

BCDs have demonstrated significant research value and promising development prospects in various fields due to their excellent physical and chemical properties and broad application potential. Notably, the application of BCDs in AZIBs exhibits a range of potential advantages [26]. First and foremost, utilizing biomass waste as a raw material not only reduces production costs but also streamlines the preparation process, thereby promoting environmental protection and resource recycling. Substituting traditional metal oxides or other hazardous materials with BCDs can substantially mitigate the risk of environmental pollution and enhance battery safety. BCDs exhibit good hydrophilicity and dispersion, it allowed them to be well-distributed in the electrolyte, which helps form a uniform electrode structure, thereby improving battery stability and cycling lifetime [27]. The surface of BCDs contains abundant functional groups (such as hydroxyl and carboxyl), indicating numerous adjustable surface chemical active sites that can interact with Zn2+ in the electrolyte to enhance adsorption and storage capacity [28,29]. Additionally, BCDs can be modified to introduce different functional groups, achieved multifunctional integration such as charge storage for designing high-performance composite materials. In summary, the use of BCDs in AZIBs has the potential to significantly improve battery performance metrics while meeting economic and presenting a promising technical path. To enhance the utilization of functional surface for BCDs, polar groups were introduced and anchored onto the Zn anode, then improved Zn2+ reaction kinetics.

Herein, we incorporated BCDs as a multipurpose additive into aqueous ZnSO4 electrolyte and systematically explored its modulating effects on a robust dendrite-free Zn metal anode. Specifically, BCDs can “buffer the redistribution effect” while considering economic cost and environmental sustainability. Consequently, symmetrical Zn||Zn cells with a BCDs-to-ZnSO4 ratio of 1:2 demonstrated an ultralong cycling life of nearly 3450 h at current densities of 1 mA cm−2 and a capacity of 1 mAh cm−2. Moreover, a full battery with the BCDs/ZnSO4 electrolyte exhibited outstanding cycling performance with a high capacity of 108.7 mAh g−1 and a high capacity retention rate even after 5000 charge/discharge cycles at a current density of 0.5 A g−1. In this work, a biomass carbon quantum dots with “buffer the redistribution effect” is proposed an electrolyte additive for advancing AZIBs the development of high-performance aqueous energy storage systems.

Based on the aforementioned analysis, we have developed an innovative BCDs as an additive to the ZnSO4 electrolyte in AZIBs, as illustrated in Fig. 1 . The incorporation of BCDs serves to homogenize the electric field, thereby rampant dendrite growth and parasitic side reactions. Experimental findings corroborated by theoretical computations indicate that the adsorption energy between metallic Zn and electrically neutral BCDs is lower than that between Zn and Zn2+. This suggests that Zn2+ ions are more prone to rapid anchoring on the surface of metallic Zn, leading to irregular structures and uneven zinc ion deposition during charge–discharge cycles, which ultimately results in dendrite formation. The addition of BCDs can effectively “buffer the redistribution effect,” specifically by trapping Zn2+ ions through SO4 2− in solution. The weak adsorption of SO4 2− on the Zn surface reduces the deposition rate of Zn2+, providing sufficient time for adsorbed Zn atoms to migrate to optimal positions on the Zn surface.

To confirm the successful preparation of BCDs, X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was performed to investigate the crystalline structure of the synthesized BCDs (Fig. 2
a). A wide peak around 23.5 was observable, suggesting the presence of an amorphous carbon structure [30]. The addition of BCDs imparts exceptional fluorescent characteristics to the electrolyte, as illustrated in Fig. 2b. Observing the PL spectra for BCDs showing a strong emission peak. In particular, BCDs/ZnSO4 have a significantly lower PL intensity compared to BCDs [31]. Moreover, in the Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra result exhibits the vibrations of –OH (3450 cm−1), −COOH (1650 cm−1) in Fig. S1 [32]. As shown in Fig. 2c, the maximum emission intensity is concentrated at 440 nm and the excitation wavelength is 360 nm. The prepared BCDs emit bright blue fluorescence under excitation of 360 nm ultraviolet light. BCDs were highly soluble in water, fluoresce brightly when exposed to UV light [33]. The energy-storing holes on the surface of carbon quantums could be the source of this photoluminescence phenomenon. The fluorescence behavior of BCDs is dependent on excitation. The BCDs exhibits a consistent distribution with an average size of 3.189 nm, as depicted in Fig. S2. This is corroborated by the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image presented in Fig. S3. Moreover, high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images show the excellent crystal properties of BCDs, featuring a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm. This value aligns closely with the d-spacing characteristic of (100) planes for graphene [31]. FTIR was employed to analyze electrolytes containing varying concentrations of BCDs. As depicted in Fig. 2d, an observable shift towards lower wave numbers is evident in the OH stretching band of H2O (3000–3500 cm−1) as the concentration of BCDs increases. This shift is attributed to the enhanced hydrogen bonding between BCDs-H2O within the system. Such observations are further validated by Raman spectra and nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy. The 2H peak for a D2O solution with 2 M ZnSO4 is presented in Fig. 2e and f. With the addition of BCDs, the peak value of Raman spectra decreased significantly, which was consistent with the results of FTIR spectra. An increase in BCDs concentration results in the 2H peak shifting towards the high field direction (exhibiting a lower chemical shift), in 2 M ZnSO4 electrolyte, the 2H peak is 3.75 ppm. When the solution was infused with BCDs. is 3.75 ppm. When BCDs was added to the solution, the 2H peak finally shifted to 3.62 ppm. This shift occurs due to a reduction in the electron cloud density surrounding D2O, along with the formation of hydrogen bonds between BCDs and D2O, which diminishes the proton shielding effect of the water molecules [3]. Consequently, the addition of BCDs leads to a restructuring of the original electrolyte system, where water molecules are more inclined to form hydrogen bonds with BCDs [34]. The theoretical study was employed in Fig. 2g. Thus, the reaction of formed Zn-SO4-5H2O and Zn-6H2O is investigated at Zn (002) surface. It is seen that the Zn-SO4-5H2O will connect to Zn (002) with O atom in SO4 2−, while the Zn-6H2O on Zn (002) is a physisorption process. However, for Zn2+ migration, both Zn-SO4-5H2O and Zn-6H2O configurations show the same trend that negligible barriers (<0.1 eV) and large exothermic process (Figs. S4 and S5). This suggests that SO4 2− show less influence to lower Zn2+ migration rate to Zn surface, as the ZnSO4 adsorption is occurred. Combining with the adsorption of SO4 2− on Zn (002) and Zn2+-SO4 2−, SO4 2− in the solution can capture Zn2+, and the weak adsorption of SO4 2− on the Zn (002) will lower the rate of Zn2+ deposition on the Zn (002), finally leaving enough time for adsorbed Zn atom moving to perfect pattern on Zn (002). But, as the SO4 2− adsorption on the Zn (002) surface, the adsorbed SO4 2− will form an equipotential system with the Zn (002), and the Zn2+ migration will become fast. For Zn (101), the same trend and conclusion are also found (Fig. S6).

Further, we performed electrochemical characterization to demonstrate the modification of the zinc anode by BCDs. Zn||Cu cells cyclic voltammetry (CV) measurements were used to examine the effect of BCDs additive on the Zn deposition kinetics, as illustrated in Fig. 3
a. Actually, with the BCDs additive exhibited a lower overpotential for Zn nucleation compared to the ZnSO4 electrolyte, suggesting that the higher reaction kinetics with aid of BCDs additive, advantageous for the homogeneous Zn deposition and the inhibition of hydrogen evolution [35]. Furthermore, in Fig. 3b the polarization potential of the Zn||Cu asymmetric in BCDs containing electrolyte (115.7 mV). In addition, as displayed in Fig. 3c, the Zn||Cu asymmetric cell achieves a long-term cycling stability of more than 250 cycles with the CE reached 97.8 % at 1 mA cm−2 with a capacity of 1 mAh cm−2. In contrast, the cell without the addition of BCD fails after 24 cycles, suggesting the unstable Zn plating/striping on the Cu foil without the addition of BCDs. To verified the above demonstration, electrochemical measurements of Zn||Zn symmetric batteries were made using 2 M ZnSO4 electrolyte and a ratio of BCDs to ZnSO4 of 1:2. The corrosion behavior was tested of Zn in the electrolyte was investigated by Tafel curve (Fig. 3d). Fortunately, in a two-electrode system, the lower current density with BCDs compared to the higher current density in ZnSO4 electrolyte indicates that BCDs as electrolyte additive was beneficial for slowing Zn corrosion. At the same time, the deposition of BCDs layer inhibits the direct contact between the aqueous electrolyte and the Zn anode during electroplating, thus enhancing the corrosion inhibition. In order to study the effect of BCDs on the physical properties and plating and stripping properties of the electrolyte, the Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) was carried out before and after the cycle. EIS measurements further revealed that the charge transfer resistance of batteries with BCDs electrolyte additives is much smaller, indicating a rapid electron transfer capability as shown in Fig. 3e [36]. We also investigated the corrosion resistance of zinc with or without BCDs electrolyte additives using linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) (Fig. 3f). Specifically, the inhibition of HER overpotential on hydrogen evolution in cells containing BCDs electrolytes was significantly higher than in pure ZnSO4 electrolytes. To verify the above hypothesis, electrochemical measurements were performed on symmetric Zn||Zn cells, galvanostatic cycling of symmetric cells at 1 mA cm−2 and 1 mAh cm−2 with 2 M ZnSO4 and ZnSO4: BCDs 1:2 electrolytes. As shown in Fig. 3g, a symmetrical Zn||Zn cell after 159 h cycling in ZnSO4 electrolyte, the polarization voltage increased significantly and breakdown occurred. Thismightbe theresultof uneven plating at random Zn nucleationsites, which leads to the fast growth of rigid dendrites. Impressively, when BCDs additive was added to the electrolyte, a stable voltage distribution of nearly 3450h can be clearly observed. This was significantly better than the performance of Zn anodes without BCDs support. 20 times more than samples without BCDs. The study found that without protective BCDs, zinc dendrite growth was unhindered, resulting in increased interfacial resistance that led to cells failure. In addition, at the higher current density of 5 mA cm−2, the symmetric cells performance with BCDs additives can operate for more than 750 h (Figs. S7 and S8). Under the same current density and capacity condition without BCDs, Zn||Zn can only work for 83 h. Moreover, the symmetric cells performance of BCDs can operate for more than 400 h at a higher current density (10 mA cm−2), and only 41 h without BCDs in Figs. S9 and S10. Fig. S11 illustrates the rate capability of the Zn||Zn cells, the cells with BCDs additive displayed a stable voltage curve from 1 mA cm−2 to 10 mA cm−2 compared to the ZnSO4 electrolyte. Various carbon-based additives for AZIBs, such as some organic additives, for example, pyridine (Py) [37]. However, the lifetime of these materials under both high and low plating/stripping conditions is significantly shorter compared to BCDs/ZnSO4. For instance, Py exhibits a lifetime of over 500 h under high current density conditions (2 mA cm−2, 6.3 mAh cm−2) and 3250 h under low current density conditions (0.5 mA cm−2, 0.5 mAh cm−2). This discrepancy can be attributed to the buffer redistribution effect where BCDs serve as a support for SO4 2−, and the linkage mechanism further extends the residence time of Zn2+ on SO4 2−-BCDs. Consequently, this phenomenon significantly enhances the stability of Zn anode plating/stripping. Other containing carbon-based additives, such as Maltitol [38], M [39], Gly [22], FCQDs [32], CHD [40], Irgacure2959 [41], SDBS [42], BSA [43], AQ [44], DASS [18], VER [45], TBATS [46] summarized in Fig. 3h. We can better comprehendthe initial nucleation process and thevariations inconcentration of electroactive substancesclose tothesurface thanks to the corresponding CA diagram. As seen in Fig. S12, the current density of the Zn electrode inthe ZnSO4 electrolyte takes a long timetostabilize because ofthe two-dimensional diffusion of Zn2+ on thesurface of Zn sheet. Ontheother hand, after a brief deposition period of roughly 50 s.TheZn anode showed a stablecurrent density in the presence of the BCDs electrolyteadditive.Thisindicatesthat Zn2+ adsorbed on the zinc sheetsurfacecan besteadilyreduced to ZnO under theinfluenceof three-dimensional diffusion.Thefindings demonstratedthat the addition of BCDs was beneficialtoensure thatthe Zn metal surfaceis uniformly depositedandthat dendriticgrowthwas inhibitedduring theis beneficial to promote the uniform deposition of Zn metal surface and inhibit the growth of dendrites during the reaction process of the cells, resulting in an excellent cyclingperformance for the Zn anode [47]. In order to understand the specific role of BCDs in electrolyte additives in more detail, we carried out theoretical calculation simulation.

In order to observe the evolution of the galvanizing process in real time, we employed an in situ optical microscope. Electroplating in the BCDs/ZnSO4 electrolytes resulted in uniform and dense Zn deposits, suggesting that inhibited dendrite growth. It can be seen from Fig. 4
a uniform Zn deposition was consistently observed in the BCDs additives throughout the plating process. In contrast, in ZnSO4 electrolyte, after electroplating at 2 mA cm−2 for 10 min, numerous nuclei or protrusions obvious protrusions appeared on the zinc surface, ultimately leading to the formation of huge protrusions and dendrites after 30 min of plating. Resulting from the free growth of non-oriented crystal faces in the ZnSO4 electrolyte (Fig. 4b). In order to further explore the effect of BCDs on Zn2+, the current density distribution, electric field distribution and Zn2+ concentration near the surface of Zn were simulated based on COMSOL as presented in Fig. 4c and d. The introduction of BCDs homogenizes the current density distribution, which in turn homogenizes the distribution of ions at the Zn electrode/electrolyte interface. The “tip effect” causes electric fields to tend to build up at these peaks in pure ZnSO4 electrolytes, which leads to dendritic development [48]. In order to promote uniform zinc deposition and the reversible striping/plating process, BCDs can reduce the “tip effect” and provide a consistent electric field distribution on the zinc surface. The diffusion of Zn2+ in BCDs is more evident at higher electric field intensities. Furthermore, compared to pure ZnSO4, the Zn2+ flow close to the Zn electrode's surface is more uniform because of the BCDs, which can lessen concentration polarization and produce uniform Zn deposition [49]. In order to promote dendritic-free behavior and homogeneous Zn deposition, BCDs may also redistribute the electric field and Zn2+ flow. These simulation results provide strong theoretical support for hypothesis that BCDs can effectively act as a homogenizing electric field, buffer the redistribution effect, promote uniform Zn deposition, thus limiting the growth of Zn dendrites and improving the battery life.

The theoretical calculation was employed to show the function of BCDs through the adsorption situation and Zn ion migration. For Zn (002), the BCDs, SO4 2− and Zn2+, which existed in solution, were all investigated and the adsorption energies are −1.62, −0.01 and −22.12 eV, respectively. For SO4 2−, the chemisorption brings weak adsorption energy of −0.01 eV. This is caused by repulsion between the Zn surfaces with rich electron and the extra electron in SO4 2− as depicted in Fig. 4e. The strongest adsorption of Zn2+ on Zn surface suggests that the Zn2+ is more favorable to rapidly anchor at the surface and easily form the irregular structures. While there are no chemical bonds for BCDs so it is suggesting a physisorption (Fig. S13). This process suggests that the BCDs will not be strongly bonded and it will be suspended above the Zn surface. As exhibited in Fig. 4f, for Zn2+ the interaction of solution molecules (SO4 2−, H2O and BCDs) was all investigated, in which the BCDs showed strong repulsion and it cannot adsorb the Zn2+ directly. For SO4 2− and H2O, a chemical adsorption structure can be formed with Zn2+ adsorption with strong adsorption energies of −17.76 (Zn-SO4 2−) and −15.70 eV (Zn-6H2O), respectively. Thus, to prevent the adsorption of SO4 2− on Zn (002), the BCDs will be working. The adsorption between SO4 2− and BCDs is chemical interaction with the energy of −1.16 eV. This suggests that the SO4 2− species can be effectively captured by CN rather than Zn (002). In addition, the physisorption of BCDs on Zn (002) make the captured SO4 2− have less opportunity to contact with the Zn surface. Thus, the SO4 2− can stably adsorb on the BCDs, meanwhile the SO4 2− can also capture the Zn2+ from solution. Such mechanism can lengthen the time of residence time of Zn2+ on SO4 2−-BCDs, which can effectively leave more time for adsorbed Zn2+ migration to the most stable site on Zn (002) and form a perfect pattern. In addition, for Zn (101), the same trend and conclusion are also found in Fig. S14. Overall, SO4 2− can capture the Zn2+ to lower the deposition rate of Zn on Zn (002), meanwhile the BCDs can act as a support for SO4 2− to further improve such function.

The discussion of by-products further illustrates the effect of BCDs additives on Zn anodes. TheXRD patternsof these by-products can be examined to confirm their existencein Fig. 5
a.Dispersed accumulation of Zn4SO4(OH)6·5H2O byproducts can be clearly observed, which severely hinders the movement of ions and electrons at the interface. Zn plating was further analyzed by SEM (Fig. S15). The Raman peaks of Zn-OSO3 2− at 210 cm−1 and Zn(OH)2 at 548 cm−1 are observed on the Zn anode cycled in ZnSO4 [50] (Fig. 5b). As for that cycled in ZnSO4/BCDs electrolyte, no significant spikes in by-products can be observed on Zn anode. Taking S 2p spectroscopy as an example, with the increase of sputtering time, the signal strength of the Zn anode S 2p spectra after 50 cycles in the ZnSO4/BCDs electrolyte gradually decreases (Fig. 5c) compared with the pure ZnSO4 electrolyte. After 50 cycles (1 mA cm−2 and 1 mAh cm−2), Zn deposition in BCDs electrolyte additive had a flat surface (Fig. S15a), while Zn deposition in ZnSO4 electrolyte had an uneven and dendritic appearance (Fig. S15c). A dense and smooth Zn layer was observed in the BCDS-containing electrolyte (Fig. S15b). On the other hand, the side-view SEM image of Zn deposition in ZnSO4 shows an uneven morphology (Fig. S15d). The formation of by-products that occur in conjunction with zinc deposition is responsible for this. Additionally, the corresponding three-dimensional height plot under the protection of BCDS does not reveal any longitudinal dendrite formation (Fig. 5d). Furthermore, confirm the presence of severe zinc dendrites, which result from the free growth of unoriented crystal planes in the ZnSO4 electrolyte (Fig. 5e). The fluorescence properties of BCDs were further investigated by CLSM to investigate Zn deposition. Confocal luminescence images of Zn deposits showing smooth and flat Zn sheets were shown in Fig. 5f after 50 cycles of ZnSO4: BCDs 2:1 at 1 mA cm−2 and 1 mAh cm−2. The time-of-flight secondary-ion mass spectrometry (TOF-SIMS) analysis was employed to further analyze the phase distribution of the by-product interface in Fig. 5g, random distribution of negative ions C−, O−, SO−, ZnO−, ZnS− and SOH− by-products was detected from the with BCDs electrolyte. The TOF-SIMS 2D depth map of the corresponding by-products was shown in Fig. S16. The addition of BCDs can buffer the redistribution effect, promote the uniform growth of zinc surface, and delay the battery life. The contact angle between the electrolyte and the Zn metal surface decreased from 65.1°to 61.5°upon the addition of the BCDs additive, as shown in Fig. S17. The BCDs can help ensure that the electrolyte is distributed uniform on the electrode surface. The Zn, O, S, and C elements are evenly distributed on the Zn electrode's surface, according to the elemental mapping in Fig. S18. The energy spectrum shown in Fig. S19 can also be used to demonstrate the existence of the elements Zn, O, S, and C. XPS measurements of C1s were carried out in order to thoroughly examine the intrinsic function mechanism of BCDs, as shown in Fig. S20. Three distinct peaks in the C 1s spectrum are attributed to CC, CO, and CS, respectively.

In order to investigate the practical application of BCDs additives, the full cells were assembled by using Zn metal anodes and Polyaniline (PANI) as cathode. PANI is a conductive organic polymer with long π-electron conjugations and is a suitable material for the biocompatible AZIBs cathode electrode [51]. The electrochemical properties of Zn||PANI battery in ZnSO4 electrolyte and BCDs/ZnSO4 electrolytes were compared. From the CV curve (Fig. 6
a), the shape can be well maintained, indicating that the electrochemical reaction mechanism of Zn||PANI batteries is not changed. The hypothesis is verified by CV tests on Zn||PANI with scanning rates ranging from 0.3 to 5 mV s−1. The CV peak value progressively widens as the scanning rate rises, but it keeps the original curve's shape (Fig. S21). After 1000 cycles at 0.5 A g−1, the Zn||PANI battery with BCDs/ZnSO4 electrolyte maintains a high capacity retention of 90.05 %. (Fig. 6b). Subsequently, the rate performance of the Zn||PANI full cell shows superior electrochemical performance as displayed in Fig. 6c, delivering specific capacities of 213.4, 196.3, 176.1, 161.2, 146.8 and 130.3 mAh g−1 at 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1, 2 and 5 A g−1, respectively. When the current density was switched back to 0.1 A g−1, the Zn||PANI full cell with BCDs-containing electrolyte still yielded a stable capacity of 211.1 mAh g−1, demonstrating the superior reversibility. The full cell with electrolyte containing BCDs has a higher capacity at different current densities than that with pure ZnSO4 electrolyte. These results indicated that the BCD additive has an impact on the reaction kinetics of the full cell system and significantly improves its stability and durability. Simultaneously, the BCDs/ZnSO4 electrolyte-based full cell also demonstrated reduced electrochemical impedance (Fig. 6d). The Zn||PANI full cell's long-cycle performance was assessed at a current density of 0.5 A g−1 (Fig. 6e). After 2000 cycles, the cell containing the ZnSO4 electrolyte experienced a short circuit, retaining only 42.6 % of its initial capacity at the end of the cycle. In contrast, when compared to pure ZnSO4 electrolyte, cells cycled in the BCDs/ZnSO4 electrolyte demonstrated remarkable cycling stability, retaining a high specific capacity of 108.7 mAh g−1 and a higher capacity retention rate even after 5000 charge/discharge. At a current density of 2 A g−1, the Zn||PANI full cell's long-term cycling performance was also examined (Fig. 6f). After 5000 cycles, the BCDs/ZnSO4 electrolyte demonstrated an impressive 58.5 % capacity retention. In contrast, the capacity of the ZnSO4 electrolyte fades quickly with capacity retention of 34.5 % after 1778 cycles, resulting from the depletion of Zn in electrolyte during long running, demonstrating the positive effect of the BCDs additive in improving the electrochemical performance of AZIBs. Attractively, the assembled Zn||PANI pouch-type cells with BCDs/ZnSO4 electrolyte can power for light-emitting diode (LED) board (Fig. S22), and the pouch cell showed an open circuit potential of 1.024 V. These results indicated that the“buffer redistribution effect”of BCDs additives in AZIBs can improve the utilization and stability of AZIBs by regulating dendrite-free electroplating and stripping and inhibiting interfacial side reactions, further confirming the feasibility in AZIBs.

In a conclusion, we have innovatively designed an environmentally friendly natural biomass carbon quantum dots (BCDs) as an electrolyte additive. BCDs possesses abundant zincophilic groups and exhibits bright fluorescence, thereby achieving dual functionalities of zinc anode protection and fluorescence monitoring. For the first time, we introduced BCDs into the ZnSO4 electrolyte, where the activity of water molecules is suppressed due to hydrogen bonding interactions between BCDs and H2O. Simultaneously, the buffering and redistribution effects of BCDs inhibit dendrite growth and promote uniform deposition. The experimental results and theoretical calculations demonstrate that the local electric field distribution is optimized by the BCDs electrolyte, which is abundant in zincophilic groups. This optimization reduces the nucleation barrier, thereby enhancing corrosion resistance. Thus reversible Zn plating/stripping 3450 h at a current density of 1 mA cm−2 and an areal capacity of 1 mAh cm−2. Furthermore, the fabricated Zn||PANI full battery demonstrates stable cycling performance for 5000 cycles at current densities of 0.5 and 5 A g−1. This study elucidates the principle of maximizing zinc utilization through solvate structural interface design and underscores the commercial potential of BCDs additives in advancing aqueous zinc-ion batteries.

Yang Yu: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation. Li Lin: Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation. Zhen-Yu Hu: Methodology, Formal analysis. Yu-Hang Liu: Investigation, Formal analysis. Lian-Shan Sun: Formal analysis, Data curation. Wan-Qiang Liu: Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Song-Lin Tian: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis, Data curation. Wei Liu: Validation, Supervision, Software. Dong Wang: Visualization, Validation, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Jilin Provincial Natural Fund (20230101205JC), Chongqing Natural Science Foundation (cstc2022jcyj-msxmX0184). The authors extend their gratitude to Shiyanjia Lab form providing invaluable assistance with the TOF-SIMS analysis.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2025.137251.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Dong, J.; Su, L.; Peng, H.; Wang, D.; Zong, H.; Wang, G.; Yang, J., Spontaneous Molecule Aggregation for Nearly Single‐Ion Conducting Sol Electrolyte to Advance Aqueous Zinc Metal Batteries: The Case of Tetraphenylporphyrin. Angewandte Chemie International Edition 2024, 63 (21), 202401441.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Zinc-based energy storage with functionalized carbon nanotube/polyaniline nanocomposite cathodes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Li, X.; Li, Y.; Xie, S.; Zhou, Y.; Rong, J.; Dong, L., Zinc-based energy storage with functionalized carbon nanotube/polyaniline nanocomposite cathodes. Chemical Engineering Journal 2022, 427, 131799.
